title: The Frontier of Investing
body:
The frontier of investing is being shaped by access, automation, and tokenisation. Retail platforms have made it easier than ever for ordinary people to buy diversified assets, while algorithmic systems have changed how markets operate at the institutional level. The result is a market structure where information spreads faster, fees are lower, and competition for edge is much more intense.

Decentralised finance and tokenised assets are trying to rebuild parts of the financial system on programmable infrastructure. That includes lending, borrowing, trading, and fractional ownership without the old reliance on a single bank or broker. The promise is broader access and faster settlement. The risks are new forms of fragility, security problems, and regulation that has not yet caught up.

AI is becoming a real research tool in investing. Models can analyse earnings calls, filings, news, supply chains, satellite data, and sentiment at a scale no human can match. The best use cases are not magical prediction, but sharper screening, faster analysis, and better risk monitoring.

The biggest long-term shift may be that more asset classes become investable by ordinary people. Private markets, infrastructure, and real-world assets are being wrapped in new structures that reduce minimums and improve access. That could democratise capital formation, but it also increases the need for clear rules and investor education.
keyPoints: Retail access and lower fees have changed market participation
DeFi aims to recreate financial services on programmable rails
Tokenisation may make illiquid assets more accessible
AI is becoming a serious tool for research and risk analysis
Market structure is changing as fast as the asset classes themselves
Regulation will shape which new investing models survive
Investors need more literacy, not less, in a more complex market
The next frontier is access plus discipline, not speculation
furtherReading: 'The Age of Cryptocurrency' by Paul Vigna and Michael J. Casey
'The Man Who Solved the Market' by Gregory Zuckerman
